**Dip your pruning shears and wash your hands in a bucket of fresh skim milk or 10% TSP powder solution between trimming each tomato plant.**
Always handle or prune sick tomato rows strictly *last* during the day so you do not carry the virus sap onto healthy rows.
tomato::tomato_mosaic_virus.safety_note: This virus is incredibly tough and survives on dry tools, old stakes, and greenhouse strings for years. If ignored during pruning, you will personally carry the virus down your rows, blistering your leaves and turning your valuable tomatoes into brown woody waste.

tomato::tomato_yellow_leaf_curl_virus.summary: Tomato Yellow Leaf Curl Virus (Tomato ka Matha Bandha Rog or Pila Murjhan) is a highly destructive viral disease carried from plant to plant by tiny, white flying insects called whiteflies. It thrives during hot, dry summer months, turning new tomato leaf edges bright yellow and curling them tightly upward into little spoon or bowl shapes. It stops the tomato plant from growing tall, bunching it up into a useless yellow dwarf bush that drops all its flowers and produces zero tomatoes.
symptoms: Young emerging leaflets on top shoots look pale light green with minor yellowing along edges
Slight upward curling or cupping of leaflet margins
Leaves turn distinctly yellow-green or lime-yellow across edges
Leaflets cup upward aggressively, looking like tiny deep bowls or spoons (cupping symptom)
Stem internodes shorten dramatically, giving top shoots a clustered appearance
organic_treatment: Spray neem oil formulations (3000 ppm) at 3-5 mL/Liter or apply fresh 5% neem seed kernel extract (NSKE) preventatively to act as a feeding deterrent
Install yellow sticky plastic cards across rows at 15-20 cards per acre to trap flying whiteflies naturally and lower vector loads
Conserve whitefly natural predators like lacewings, ladybird beetles, and mirid bugs in chemical-free field boundary buffer zones
chemical_treatment: Imidacloprid or Thiamethoxam (Systemic Insecticide (Neonicotinoid)) — 0.4 - 0.5 mL/Id of water (or local product dependent)
prevention: Pull out and bury any young tomato plants that show bright yellow edges and upward spoon cupping early in the season.
Hang bright yellow sticky plastic cards across your rows to catch and glue flying whiteflies naturally.
Spray a targeted whitefly-killing systemic insecticide or neem oil immediately to stop insects from spreading the virus further.
tomato::tomato_yellow_leaf_curl_virus.safety_note: This is a viral infection; chemicals cannot cure it once inside the tomato plant. If infected early in the vegetative stage, the plant will become 100% sterile and produce zero yield, causing total crop failure on those lines.

wheat::healthy.summary: The leaf looks healthy — no supported disease pattern detected.
prevention: Continue regular field scouting.
Keep irrigation balanced; avoid prolonged leaf wetness.
Remove crop residue after harvest.
wheat::healthy.safety_note: No treatment needed. Re-check if new spots, curling, or yellowing appear.

wheat::septoria.summary: A fungus that causes brown rectangular spots with black specks on leaves, reducing yield if it reaches the top leaves.
symptoms: Small, chlorotic (yellow) spots on lower leaves.
Spots expand into irregular, light tan to reddish-brown lesions, often rectangular/elongated, restricted by veins.
organic_treatment: None highly effective.
Stubble management.
N/A
chemical_treatment: Propiconazole + Azoxystrobin (Fungicide) — 1mL/L
prevention: Apply a recommended fungicide if the weather is rainy.
wheat::septoria.safety_note: Protecting the flag leaf is critical for yield.

wheat::stripe_rust.summary: A highly destructive fungus causing yellow powdery stripes on leaves.
symptoms: Small, bright yellow to orange-yellow powdery pustules on leaves.
Pustules align to form narrow, linear stripes parallel to leaf veins.
organic_treatment: Not highly effective.
None once infected.
N/A
chemical_treatment: Tebuconazole (Fungicide) — 1mL/L
prevention: Spray an approved triazole fungicide immediately.
